course_info.title: College Mathematics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

learning_objectives: Define and perform operations on sets: union, intersection, complement
Construct truth tables for logical propositions
Distinguish between inductive and deductive reasoning in mathematics

lecture_notes.introduction: Mathematics is not just computation. It is a system of logical reasoning. This course builds mathematical thinking skills that apply far beyond the classroom, starting with the language of sets and logic.
key_concepts: Set notation: roster and set-builder forms
Union, intersection, difference, and complement operations
Venn diagrams for visualizing set relationships
Propositions, negation, conjunction, and disjunction
Truth tables for compound propositions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solving a real-world scheduling problem using set intersection and Venn diagrams

review.value: Union (A ∪ B) combines elements from both sets; Intersection (A ∩ B) includes only elements in both; Complement (A') is everything not in A
Systematic listing of all possible truth values for a compound proposition; each row represents one combination of truth values for component propositions

learning_objectives: Classify numbers within the real number system
Apply divisibility rules and find prime factorizations
Compute greatest common divisor and least common multiple

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding number systems and their properties provides the foundation for all quantitative reasoning. Number theory, one of the oldest branches of mathematics, has surprising modern applications in encryption and computer science.
key_concepts: Natural numbers, integers, rationals, irrationals, and reals
Prime numbers and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
GCD and LCM using prime factorization
Modular arithmetic basics
Applications of number theory in cryptography
lecture_notes.demonstration: Finding the GCD and LCM of large numbers using prime factorization trees

review.value: Every integer greater than 1 can be expressed as a unique product of prime numbers (up to ordering)
GCD: product of shared prime factors at lowest power. LCM: product of all prime factors at highest power. GCD(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b

learning_objectives: Solve linear equations and inequalities in one variable
Translate word problems into algebraic expressions
Graph linear equations on the coordinate plane

lecture_notes.introduction: Algebra is the language of generalization. Instead of solving one specific problem, algebra lets us express and solve entire families of problems at once.
key_concepts: Variables, expressions, and equations
Properties of equality: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division
Solving linear equations: isolate the variable
Graphing y = mx + b: slope and y-intercept
Translating English sentences into algebraic equations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Modeling a real-world break-even problem: how many units must a small business sell to cover costs?

review.value: y = mx + b where m is the slope (rate of change) and b is the y-intercept (starting value)
Define variables, identify relationships, write equation, solve, check answer in context of the original problem

learning_objectives: Define a function and determine if a relation is a function
Evaluate functions and interpret function notation
Identify key features of graphs: domain, range, intercepts

lecture_notes.introduction: Functions are the central concept in modern mathematics. Every time one quantity depends on another, a function is at work. Understanding functions is essential for interpreting data, modeling relationships, and making predictions.
key_concepts: Definition: a function assigns exactly one output to each input
Function notation: f(x) and its meaning
Vertical line test for graphs
Domain and range: natural restrictions and context
Linear, quadratic, and exponential function families
lecture_notes.demonstration: Graphing and comparing linear, quadratic, and exponential growth using population, investment, and distance examples

review.value: A relation where each input (x-value) produces exactly one output (y-value). Passes the vertical line test on a graph.
Domain: all valid inputs. Range: all possible outputs. Context matters: a time function has domain t ≥ 0

learning_objectives: Understand the time value of money
Analyze loan amortization and monthly payment calculations
readings: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau loan calculator guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial literacy requires mathematical literacy. Understanding interest, loans, and investments protects you from predatory lending and empowers informed financial decisions.
key_concepts: Simple interest: I = Prt
Compound interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)
Annual percentage rate (APR) vs. annual percentage yield (APY)
Loan amortization: how payments split between principal and interest
The rule of 72 for estimating doubling time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing a 15-year vs. 30-year mortgage: total interest paid, monthly payments, and equity buildup over time

review.value: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) where P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compounding frequency, t = years. More frequent compounding yields more interest.
Divide 72 by the annual interest rate to estimate years to double. At 6%, money doubles in approximately 12 years.

learning_objectives: Apply the fundamental counting principle, permutations, and combinations
Calculate basic probabilities for simple and compound events
Distinguish between independent and dependent events

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability quantifies uncertainty. From weather forecasts to medical diagnoses to game strategy, understanding probability helps us make better decisions under uncertainty.
key_concepts: Fundamental counting principle: multiply choices at each stage
Permutations: order matters, P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!
Combinations: order does not matter, C(n,r) = n!/[r!(n-r)!]
Probability of an event: P(E) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes
Independent vs. dependent events and conditional probability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating lottery odds and comparing them to everyday risks to illustrate probability intuition

review.value: Permutation: choosing and arranging (order matters). Combination: choosing without arrangement (order does not matter). 'Does the order I pick them in change the outcome?'
Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the probability of the other. P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) only when independent.

questions.question: Given A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and B = {3, 4, 5, 6, 7}, find A ∪ B, A ∩ B, and A - B. Then draw a Venn diagram showing all three sets with the universal set U = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}.
questions.answer: A ∪ B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}. A ∩ B = {3, 4, 5}. A - B = {1, 2}. Venn diagram: left circle only has {1, 2}, overlap has {3, 4, 5}, right circle only has {6, 7}, outside both circles has {8}.

rubric.key: Correct Venn diagram with all regions labeled
Element 8 correctly placed outside both circles

questions.question: Solve the equation 3(2x - 4) + 7 = 5x + 1 and verify your answer by substitution.
questions.answer: 3(2x - 4) + 7 = 5x + 1. Distribute: 6x - 12 + 7 = 5x + 1. Combine: 6x - 5 = 5x + 1. Subtract 5x: x - 5 = 1. Add 5: x = 6. Verification: 3(2(6) - 4) + 7 = 3(8) + 7 = 31. 5(6) + 1 = 31. Both sides equal 31, so x = 6 is correct.

questions.question: You invest $5,000 at 4.5% annual interest compounded monthly. How much will you have after 10 years? How much total interest did you earn? Show the formula and calculation.

rubric.key: Correct formula identification and variable substitution

questions.question: Given f(x) = 2x² - 3x + 1, evaluate f(4) and f(-2). Then determine whether the point (1, 0) is on the graph of f.

rubric.key: Correct verification that (1,0) is on the graph

major_assignments.title: Personal Financial Analysis Project
major_assignments.description: Apply the mathematical concepts from this course to create a comprehensive personal financial plan. Using real-world interest rates and financial products, compare savings strategies, analyze loan options, and create a budget model using algebraic functions.
objectives: Apply compound interest formulas to real savings and investment scenarios
Compare loan options using amortization calculations
Model income and expenses using linear and piecewise functions
Demonstrate mathematical literacy in personal finance decisions
deliverables: Savings comparison: analyze 3 real savings accounts with different compounding (5-year and 10-year projections)
Loan analysis: compare 2 auto loan or student loan options with amortization tables
Budget model: linear function modeling monthly income vs. expenses with break-even analysis
Written report (1000-1500 words) explaining all calculations and financial conclusions
All formulas shown with work, not just calculator outputs

criteria: Correct compound interest calculations for all three accounts
Meaningful comparison using real interest rates
Clear explanation of how compounding frequency affects returns
Projection tables or graphs for 5-year and 10-year periods
Correct amortization calculations
Total interest comparison between loan options
Discussion of trade-offs between lower payment and total cost
Real-world interest rates and terms used
Realistic income and expense categories
Linear function correctly models the relationship
Break-even point identified and interpreted
Graph of budget function included
All calculations clearly shown with formulas
Financial conclusions are sound and well-reasoned
Written explanations demonstrate mathematical understanding
Professional formatting and error-free writing
Comprehensive financial analysis using real-world data and correct mathematics
Clear demonstration of compound interest, amortization, and function modeling
Well-written report that connects mathematical calculations to practical financial decisions
All work shown with formulas, not just final answers

homework.description: Weekly problem sets reinforcing computational and reasoning skills

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of mathematical concepts and problem-solving ability

major_project.description: Personal financial analysis project applying course mathematics to real-world decisions

midterm_exam.description: Comprehensive exam covering sets, logic, algebra, and functions

final_exam.description: Cumulative final exam covering all course material

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is strongly encouraged. Students who miss class are responsible for obtaining notes and completing all assignments on time.
course_policies.late_submissions: Homework is due at the start of class. Late homework receives 50% credit for one day late and zero after that. The lowest homework score is dropped.
course_policies.collaboration: Students may discuss homework approaches but must write up solutions independently. Copying solutions is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All exams and quizzes are individual work. Use of unauthorized calculators, phones, or notes during exams will result in a zero and referral to the Dean.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Scientific calculator required (graphing calculator recommended). Access to Desmos or similar graphing tool for homework and projects.
